concepts.title: Integral and Integrand
concepts.conceptBody:
Integration, integrand and integral 

The process of finding integral of a function is called integration and the function which is integrated is called the integrand. The function whose d.c. is the integrand is called the integral of the integrand.

concepts.title: Distinction between Definite Integral and Indefinite Integral
concepts.conceptBody:
Distinction between Definite Integral and Indefinite Integral

Indefinite Integral : If $\frac{\text{d}\left\{ \text{F}\left( \text{x} \right) \right\}}{\text{dx}}=\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ then also $\frac{\text{d}\left\{ \text{F}\left( \text{x} \right)+\text{c} \right\}}{\text{dx}}=\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ where $\text{c}$ is an arbitrary constant.

Thus here general value of $\int f\left( x \right)dx$ is $F\left( x \right)+c$, where $c$ is an arbitrary constant. c is called constant of integration.

Clearly integral will change if c changes. Thus integral of a function is not unique and if one integral of $f\left( x \right)$ is $F\left( x \right)$, then $F\left( x \right)+c$ will be also an integral of $f\left( x \right)$, where $c$ is a constant.

Thus $\int f\left( x \right)dx$ will have infinite number of values and hence it is called indefinite integral of $f\left( x \right)$.

Definition Particular Integral : If $\frac{d\left[ F\left( x \right)+c \right]}{dx}=f\left( x \right)$ then

$\int f\left( x \right)dx=F\left( x \right)+c\cdot \int f\left( x \right)dx$ will have different values for different values of $c$.

For a definite value ${{C}_{1}}$ of $c,\int f\left( x \right)dx$ will have a definite value $F\left( x \right)+{{c}_{1}}$.

 

Hence $F\left( x \right)+{{c}_{1}}$ is a particular integral of $f\left( x \right)$.

Note: Because of constant of integration it is possible that the same function may have different integrals but the difference between two integrals of a function is a constant.

Thus, an indefinite integral is a family of functions $\text{y}=\text{F}\left( \text{x} \right)+\text{C}$.

From the geometrical point of view, an indefinite integral is a collection (family) of curves, each of which is obtained by translating one of the curves parallel to itself upwards or downwards (that is, along the y-axis). For example, if you graph the three antiderivatives of $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=2\text{x}$, you will see that the all graphs have the same shape because at any x-value, their tangent lines all have the same slope, as shown in figure. A natural question arises : do antiderivatives (and, hence, indefinite integrals) exist for every function $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ ? The answer is no. Let us note, however, without proof, that if a function, $\text{f}\left( \text{X} \right)$ is continuous on an interval $\left[ a,b \right]$, then this function has an antiderivative and hence, there is also an indefinite integral).

This chapter is devoted to working out methods by means of which we can find antiderivatives (and indefinite integrals) for certain classes of elementary functions.

concepts.title: Elementary Integrals
concepts.conceptBody:
Table of standard elementary integrals 

The table of standard integrals has been given below which is based on d.c. of elementry functions:

(i) $\frac{\text{d}}{\text{dx}}\left( \text{x} \right)=1$ $\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \text{d}x=\text{x}$

(ii) $\frac{d}{dx}\left( \frac{{{x}^{n+1}}}{n+1} \right)={{x}^{n}}$ $\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int {{x}^{n}}dx=\frac{{{x}^{n+1}}}{n+1}\left( n\ne -1 \right)$

(iii) $\frac{\text{d}}{\text{dx}}\left( \text{log}\left| \text{x} \right| \right)=\frac{1}{\text{x}}$ $\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \frac{1}{\text{x}}\text{dx}=\text{logIx}\mid $

(iv) $\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{sin}x \right)=\text{cos}x$ $\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \text{cos}dx=\text{sin}x$

(v) $\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{cos}x \right)=-\text{sin}x$ $\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \text{sin}xdx=-\text{cos}x$

(vi) $\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{tan}x \right)=\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}x$ $\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \text{se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}\text{xdx}=\text{tanx}$

(vii) $\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{cot}x \right)=-\text{cose}{{\text{c}}^{2}}x$ $\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \text{cose}{{\text{c}}^{2}}\text{xd}x=-\text{cotx}$

(viii) $\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{sec}x \right)=\text{sec}x\text{tan}x$ $\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \text{secxtanxdx}=\text{secx}$

(ix) $\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{cosec}x \right)=-\text{cosec}x\text{cot}x$ $\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \text{cosec}x\text{cot}xdx=-\text{cosec}x$

(x) $\frac{d}{dx}\left( {{e}^{x}} \right)={{e}^{x}}$ $\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int {{\text{e}}^{\text{x}}}\text{dx}={{\text{e}}^{\text{x}}}$

(xi) $\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}x \right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-{{x}^{2}}}}$ $\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-{{x}^{2}}}}dx=\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}x$

(xii) $\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}x \right)=\frac{1}{1+{{x}^{2}}}$ $\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+{{\text{x}}^{2}}}}\text{dx}=\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}$

(xiii) $\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{se}{{\text{c}}^{-1}}x \right)=\frac{1}{x\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}-1}}$ $\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \frac{1}{\text{x}\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}-1}}\text{dx}=\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{-1}}\text{x}$

(xiv) $\frac{d}{dx}\left( {{a}^{x}} \right)={{a}^{x}}\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{e}}a$ $\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int {{a}^{x}}dx=\frac{{{a}^{x}}}{\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{e}}a},a>0$

Other trigonometric integrals

$~\int ~\text{tan}xdx=\text{log}\left| \text{sec}x \right|+c$

$~\int ~\text{cot}xdx=\text{log}\left| \text{sin}x \right|+c$

$~\int ~\text{sec}xdx=\text{log}\left| \text{sec}x+\text{tan}x \right|+c=\text{log}\left| \text{tan}\left( \frac{\pi }{4}+\frac{x}{2} \right) \right|+c$

$~\int ~\text{cosec}xdx=\text{log}\left| \text{cosec}x-\text{cot}x \right|+c=\left| \text{tan}{{x}_{2}} \right|+c$

We will do the proofs of these later.

The formulas should be remembered by hearted.

concepts.title: Important Points necessary to kept in mind while performing integration
concepts.conceptBody:
Some Properties of the Indefinite Integral 

Rule 

  1. The indefinite integral of an algebraic sum of two or more functions is equal to algebraic sum of their integrals.

$\int ~\left[ {{f}_{1}}\left( x \right)\pm {{f}_{2}}\left( x \right) \right]dx=\int ~{{f}_{1}}\left( x \right)dx\pm \int ~{{f}_{2}}\left( x \right)dx$

  1. Constant factor may be taken outside the integral sign, that is, if a const, then

$\int ~\text{af}\left( x \right)dx=a\int ~f\left( x \right)dx$

When evaluating indefinite integrals it is useful to bear in mind the following rules.

Example: 

$\int ~\left( 2{{x}^{3}}-3\text{sin}x+5\sqrt{x} \right)=\int ~2{{x}^{3}}dx-\int ~3\text{sin}xdx+\int ~5\sqrt{x}dx$

  • Here we have divided the L.HS. into separate integrals on R.H.S. and also we have taken constants outside the integration.

$~=2\int ~{{x}^{3}}dx-3\int ~\text{sin}xdx+5\int ~{{x}^{1/2}}dx$

$~=2\frac{{{x}^{3+1}}}{3+1}-3\left( -\text{cos}x \right)+5\frac{{{x}^{1/2+1}}}{\frac{1}{2}+1}+C$

$~=\frac{1}{2}{{x}^{4}}+3\text{cos}x+\frac{10}{3}x\sqrt{x}+C$

Example: 

$\int ~\left( \frac{3}{\sqrt[3]{\text{x}}}+\frac{1}{2\sqrt{\text{x}}}+\text{x}\sqrt[4]{\text{x}} \right)=$$3\int ~{{\text{x}}^{-1/3}}\text{dx}+\frac{1}{2}\int ~{{\text{x}}^{-1/2}}\text{dx}+\int ~{{\text{x}}^{5/4}}\text{dx}$

 

=$3\frac{{{\text{x}}^{-\frac{1}{3}+1}}}{-\frac{1}{3}+1}+\frac{1}{2}\frac{{{\text{x}}^{-\frac{1}{2}+1}}}{-\frac{1}{2}+1}+\frac{{{\text{x}}^{\frac{5}{4}+1}}}{\frac{5}{4}+1}=\frac{9}{2}\sqrt[3]{\text{x}}+\sqrt{\text{x}}+\frac{4}{9}{{\text{x}}^{2}}\sqrt[4]{\text{x}}+\text{C}$

  1. If $\int f\left( x \right)dx=F\left( x \right)+C$ then $\int f\left( x+b \right)dx=F\left( x+b \right)+C$
  2. $\int f\left( x \right)dx=F\left( x \right)+C$ then $\int f\left( ax+b \right)dx=\frac{1}{a}\mathbf{F}\left( \mathbf{ax}+b \right)+\mathbf{C}$

for example

(i) if $\int \text{cos}\left( 3x+5 \right)dx=\frac{\text{sin}\left( 3x+5 \right)}{3}$

(ii) $\int {{\text{e}}^{\text{x}}}\text{dx}={{\text{e}}^{\text{x}}}$, then $\int {{\text{e}}^{4\text{x}-5}}\text{dx}=\frac{{{\text{e}}^{4\text{x}-5}}}{4}$

(iii) $\int {{\text{x}}^{\text{n}}}\text{dx}=\frac{{{\text{x}}^{\text{n}+1}}}{\text{n}+1}$, then $\int \sqrt{2\text{x}+3}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ dx}=\frac{1}{2}\frac{{{(2\text{x}+3)}^{1/2+1}}}{\left( 1/2+1 \right)}=\frac{1}{3}{{(2\text{x}+3)}^{3/2}}$

(iv) $\int \frac{\text{dx}}{\text{x}+3}=\text{ln}\left| \text{x}+3 \right|+\text{C}$

(v) $\int \text{cos}7xdx=\frac{1}{7}\text{sin}7x+C$

(vi) $\int \text{sin}\left( 2x-6 \right)dx=-\frac{1}{2}\text{cos}\left( 2x-6 \right)+C$

concepts.title: Integration of Algebraic functions expressible as the sum of some other Integrable Algebraic functions
concepts.conceptBody:
Method of transformation

In this method of integration, given function is expressed as the algebraic sum of such functions whose integral can be written down with the help of standard formulae.

Integration of Algebraic functions 

Rule: 
Simplify the given function whose integral is to be found to express it as the algebraic sum of functions of the following forms 
(i) ${{(\text{ax}+\text{b})}^{\text{n}}}$ (ii) $\frac{1}{1+{{\text{x}}^{2}}}$ 
(iii) $\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-{{\text{x}}^{2}}}}$ (iv) $\frac{1}{\text{x}\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}-1}}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (a) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Find }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \left( 4{{x}^{3}}+3{{x}^{2}}+2x+4 \right)dx & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (b) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Find }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \sqrt{x}\left( {{x}^{\frac{3}{2}}}-7{{x}^{\frac{5}{2}}}+9{{x}^{\frac{7}{2}}} \right)dx \\ \end{array}$

(c) Find $\int \frac{{{(1+x)}^{3}}}{\sqrt{x}}dx$

Solution: 

(a) As the rule says, we will break the integral into sum of different interals and will then use standard formulas.

$~\int ~\left( 4{{x}^{3}}+3{{x}^{2}}+2x+4 \right)dx$

$~=\int ~4{{x}^{3}}dx+\int ~3{{x}^{2}}dx+\int ~2xdx+\int ~4dx$

$~=4\int ~{{x}^{3}}dx+3\int ~{{x}^{2}}dx+2\int ~xdx+4\int ~{{x}^{0}}dx$

$~=4\cdot \frac{{{x}^{3}}+1}{3+1}+3\cdot \frac{{{x}^{2}}+1}{2+1}+2\cdot \frac{{{x}^{1+1}}}{1+1}+4\cdot \frac{{{x}^{0+1}}}{0+1}+c$

$~=4\cdot \frac{{{x}^{4}}}{4}+3\cdot \frac{{{x}^{3}}}{3}+2\cdot \frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2}+4\cdot x+c$

$~={{x}^{4}}+{{x}^{3}}+{{x}^{2}}+4x+c$

(b) $\int \sqrt{\text{x}}\left( {{\text{x}}^{\frac{3}{2}}}-7{{\text{x}}^{\frac{5}{2}}}+9{{\text{x}}^{\frac{7}{2}}} \right)\text{dx}$

$\left.=\int x^{\frac{1}{2}}\left(x^{\frac{3}{2}}-7 x^{\frac{5}{2}}+9 x^{\frac{7}{2}}\right) d x=\int x^{\frac{1}{2}} \cdot x^{\frac{3}{2}}-7 x^{\frac{1}{2}} \cdot x^{\frac{5}{2}}+9 x^{\frac{1}{2}} \cdot x^{\frac{7}{2}}\right) d x$

$=\int\left(x^2-7 x^3+9 x^4\right) d x=\int x^2 d x-7 \int x^3 d x+9 \int x^4 d x$

$=\frac{x^2+1}{2+1}-7 \cdot \frac{x^3+1}{3+1}+9 \cdot \frac{x^4+1}{4+1}+c$

$=\frac{x^3}{3}-7 \cdot \frac{x^4}{4}+9 \cdot \frac{x^5}{5}+c$

$\text { (c) } \int \frac{(1+x)^3}{\sqrt{x}} d x$

$= \int \frac{1+3 x+3 x^2+x^3}{\sqrt{x}} d x=\int\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}+\frac{3 x}{\sqrt{x}}+\frac{3 x^2}{\sqrt{x}}+\frac{x^3}{\sqrt{x}}\right) d x$

$= \int\left(x^{-\frac{1}{2}}+3 x^{1-\frac{1}{2}}+3 x^{2-\frac{1}{2}}+x^{3-\frac{1}{2}}\right) d x$

$= \int x^{-\frac{1}{2}} d x+3 \int x^{\frac{1}{2}} d x+3 \int x^{\frac{3}{2}} d x+\int x^{\frac{5}{2}} d x$

$= \int \frac{x^{\frac{1}{2}}}{\frac{1}{2}}+3 \frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}+3 \cdot \frac{x^{\frac{5}{2}}}{\frac{5}{2}}+\frac{x^{\frac{7}{2}}}{\frac{7}{2}}+c=2 \sqrt{x}+2 x^{\frac{3}{2}}+\frac{6}{5} x^{\frac{5}{2}}+\frac{2}{7} x^{\frac{7}{2}}+c$

concepts.title: Integration done by expressing the numerator in accordance with the denominator
concepts.conceptBody:
Expressing numerator in terms of denominator 

Q) (a) $\int \frac{{{x}^{4}}+1}{{{x}^{2}}+1}dx$ 

Here we will transform the numerator, such that it can be expressed in terms of denominator. ${{\text{x}}^{4}}+1=\left( {{\text{x}}^{4}}-1 \right)+2=\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}-1 \right)\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}+1 \right)+2$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \frac{{{\text{x}}^{4}}+1}{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+1}\text{dx}=\int \frac{\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}-1 \right)\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}+1 \right)+2}{\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}+1 \right)}\text{dx}$

$=\int \left( {{x}^{2}}-1 \right)dx+2\int \frac{dx}{\left( {{x}^{2}}+1 \right)}$

$=\frac{{{x}^{3}}}{3}-x+2\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}x+c$

(b) $\int \frac{ax+b}{cx+d}dx$ 

This is a general form of type $\frac{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Linear }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Linear }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}$, you should follow this example very closely.

Again we will express numerator in terms of denominator.

$ax+b$ $~=\frac{a}{c}\left[ \left( cx+d \right)-\left( d-\frac{bc}{a} \right] \right.$

$\therefore $ $\int ~\frac{ax+b}{cx+d}dx$ $~=\frac{a}{c}\int ~\frac{\left( x+d \right)-\left( d-\frac{bc}{a} \right)}{cx+d}dx$

$~=\frac{a}{c}\left[ \int ~dx-\left( d-\frac{bc}{a} \right)\int ~{{(cx+d)}^{-1}}dx \right.$

$~=\frac{a}{c}\left( x-\left( \frac{d-bc}{a} \right)\frac{1}{c}\text{log}\left| cx+d \right| \right)+c$

(c) $\int \frac{{{x}^{7}}}{x+1}dx$ 

Now here, only ${{x}^{7}}$ is in numerator, somehow we have to express it in terms of denominator.

${{x}^{7}}$ $~=\left( {{x}^{7}}+1 \right)-1$

$~=\left( x+1 \right)\left( {{x}^{6}}-{{x}^{5}}+{{x}^{4}}-{{x}^{3}}+{{x}^{2}}-x+1 \right)-1$

(using expansion of ${{x}^{7}}+1$ )

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int ~\frac{{{x}^{7}}}{x+1}$ $~=\int ~\left( {{x}^{6}}-{{x}^{5}}+{{x}^{4}}-{{x}^{3}}+{{x}^{2}}-x+1 \right)dx-\int ~\frac{dx}{x+1}$

$~=\frac{{{x}^{7}}}{7}-\frac{{{x}^{6}}}{6}+\frac{{{x}^{5}}}{5}-\frac{{{x}^{4}}}{4}+\frac{{{x}^{3}}}{3}-\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2}+x-\text{log}\left| x+1 \right|+c$

(d) $\int \frac{x}{{{(2x+1)}^{2}}}dx$ 

$\text{x}=\frac{1}{2}\left[ \left( 2\text{x}+1 \right)-1 \right]$

$\therefore $ $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int ~\frac{\text{x}}{{{(2\text{x}+1)}^{2}}}\text{dx}$ $~=\frac{1}{2}\left[ \int ~\frac{2\text{x}+1}{{{(2\text{x}+1)}^{2}}}\text{dx}-\int ~\frac{\text{dx}}{{{(2\text{x}+1)}^{2}}} \right]$

$~=\left[ \frac{1}{2}\text{log}\left| 2\text{x}+1 \right|-\frac{1}{2}\left( -\frac{1}{2\text{x}+1} \right) \right]=\frac{1}{4}\left[ \text{log}\left| 2\text{x}+1 \right|+\frac{1}{2\text{x}+1} \right]$

concepts.title: Expressing one function in form of the other function by suitable algebraic transformations when the algebraic integrand is of the type f(x).g(x)
concepts.conceptBody:
Expressing one function in form of the other function by suitable algebraic transformations when the algebraic integrand is of the type f(x).g(x)

Type III : $\int f\left( x \right)\times g\left( x \right)dx$ 

Q) Expressing one function in terms of other function 

(a) Find $\int \frac{3x+5}{\sqrt{7x+9}}dx$. 
(b) $\int \left( x-1 \right)\sqrt{x+1}dx$ 

Solution: 

(a) [Here we will write $3x+5$ in the form $a\left( 7x+9 \right)+b]$

Let $3\text{x}+5=\text{a}\left( 7\text{x}+9 \right)+\text{b}$

or $3\text{x}+5=7\text{ax}+9\text{a}+\text{b}$

Equating the coefficients of similar powers of $x$ from both sides,

we get $7\text{a}=3\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ a}=\frac{3}{7}$

and $9a+b=5\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }b=5-9a=5-9\cdot \frac{3}{7}=\frac{8}{7}$

Hence from (i), $3x+5=\frac{3}{7}\left( 7x+9 \right)+\frac{8}{7}$

Now $\int \frac{3x+5}{\sqrt{7x+9}}dx=\int \frac{\frac{3}{7}\left( 7x+9 \right)+\frac{8}{7}}{\sqrt{7x+9}}dx$

$=\frac{3}{7}\cdot \int \frac{7\text{x}+9}{\sqrt{7\text{x}+9}}\text{dx}+\frac{8}{7}\int \frac{\text{dx}}{\sqrt{7\text{x}+9}}$

$=\frac{3}{7}\cdot \int \sqrt{7\text{x}+9}\text{dx}+\frac{8}{7}\int {{(7\text{x}+9)}^{-\frac{1}{2}}}\text{dx}$

$=\frac{3}{7}\cdot \int \frac{{{(7\text{x}+9)}^{\frac{1}{2}+1}}}{\left( \frac{1}{2}+1 \right)\cdot 7}+\frac{8}{7}\cdot \frac{{{(7\text{x}+9)}^{-\frac{1}{2}+1}}}{\left( -\frac{1}{2}+1 \right)7}+\text{c}=\frac{2}{49}{{(7\text{x}+9)}^{\frac{3}{2}}}+\frac{16}{49}{{(7\text{x}+9)}^{\frac{1}{2}}}+\text{c}$

$=\frac{2}{49}\sqrt{7x+9}\left( 7x+9+8 \right)+c=\frac{2}{49}\left( 7x+17 \right)\sqrt{7x+9}+c$.

(b) $4+\text{x}-1=\text{a}\left( \text{x}+1 \right)+\text{b}$

or $\left( \text{x}-1 \right)=\text{ax}+\text{a}+\text{b}$

Equating the co-efficients of similar powers of $x$, we get

$\text{a}=1$ and $\text{a}+\text{b}=-1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\therefore \text{b}=-1-\text{a}=-2$

$\therefore $ from (i), $\text{x}-1=\left( \text{x}+1 \right)-2$

Now $\int \left( x-1 \right)\sqrt{x+1}dx=\int \left[ \left( x+1 \right)-2 \right]\sqrt{x+1}dx$

$=\int {{(\text{x}+1)}^{\frac{3}{2}}}-2\sqrt{\text{x}+1]}\text{dx}=\int \left[ \left( \text{x}+1 \right)-2 \right]\sqrt{\text{x}+1}\text{dx}$

$=\frac{{{(\text{x}+1)}^{\frac{5}{2}}}}{\frac{5}{2}}-2\cdot \frac{{{(\text{x}+1)}^{\frac{3}{2}}}}{\frac{3}{2}}+\text{c}$

$=\frac{2}{5}{{(\text{x}+1)}^{\frac{5}{2}}}-\frac{4}{3}{{(\text{x}+1)}^{\frac{3}{2}}}+\text{c}=2{{(\text{x}+1)}^{\frac{3}{2}}}\left( \frac{\text{x}+1}{5}-\frac{2}{3} \right)+\text{c}$

$=\frac{2}{5}{{(\text{x}+1)}^{\frac{5}{2}}}\cdot \frac{\left( 3\text{x}+3-10 \right)}{15}+\text{c}=\frac{2}{15}{{(\text{x}+1)}^{\frac{3}{2}}}\left( 3\text{x}-7 \right)+\text{c}$

concepts.title: Integration of Exponential Functions of form a^(mx+n)
concepts.conceptBody:
Integration of Exponential Functions of form a^(mx+n)

Type IV Integration of Exponential Functions: 

Working Rule: 

Express ${{a}^{mx+n}}$ as $b{{e}^{kx}}$ and then find the integral.

Q) Evaluate $\int {{a}^{3x+3}}dx,a>0$ 

Solution: 

$\int ~{{a}^{3x+3}}dx$ $~=\int ~{{a}^{3x}}\cdot {{a}^{3}}dx$

=${{a}^{3}}\int ~{{a}^{3x}}dx={{a}^{3}}\cdot \int ~{{e}^{3x\text{log}a}}dx\left[ \because {{a}^{x}}={{e}^{x\text{log}a}} \right]$

=${{a}^{3}}\int ~{{e}^{\left( 3x\text{log}a \right)x}}dx={{a}^{3}}\cdot \frac{{{e}^{\left( 3\text{log}a \right)x}}}{3\text{log}a}+c\left[ \because \int ~{{e}^{kx}}dx=\frac{{{e}^{kx}}}{k} \right]$

=${{a}^{3}}\cdot \frac{{{e}^{\left( 3\text{log}a \right)x}}}{3\text{log}a}+c\left[ \because \int ~{{e}^{kx}}dx=\frac{{{e}^{kx}}}{k} \right]$

=$\frac{{{a}^{3x+3}}}{3\text{log}a}+c$

Note $:\int {{a}^{x}}dx=\frac{{{a}^{x}}}{\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{e}}a}\therefore \int {{a}^{3x}}dx=\int {{\left( {{a}^{3}} \right)}^{x}}dx=\frac{{{\left( {{a}^{3}} \right)}^{x}}}{\text{log}{{a}^{3}}}+c=\frac{{{a}^{3x}}}{3\text{log}a}+c$

concepts.title: Integration of Trigonometric functions having powers of sin,cos,tan or containing expressions of from (cos mx)(sin nx)
concepts.conceptBody:
Integration of Trigonometric functions having powers of sin,cos,tan or containing expressions of from (cos mx)(sin nx) 

Type V. Integration of Trigonometrical functions using transformation method 

Working Rule 

  1. Whenever the expression is given in terms of powers of sin or cos or tan etc, convert it to the form $\text{sink}\theta ,\text{cosk}\theta ,\text{tan}\theta $ etc using following formulas 
    (i) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\text{x}=\frac{1-\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\text{x}}{2}$ (ii) $\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\text{x}=\frac{1+\text{cos}2\text{x}}{2}$ 
    (iii) $\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{3}}x=\frac{3\text{sin}x-\text{sin}3x}{4}$ (iv) $\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{3}}x=\frac{3\text{cos}x+\text{cos}3x}{4}$ 
    (v) $\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\text{x}=\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}\text{x}-1$ (vi) $\text{co}{{\text{t}}^{2}}x=\text{cose}{{\text{c}}^{2}}x-1$
  2. For the expressions of type $\text{sin}mx\text{cos}nx$, use the following formulas to get 2 separate functions & then so we the question. 
    (i) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2\text{sinAsinB}=\text{cos}\left( \text{A}-\text{B} \right)-\text{cos}\left( \text{A}+\text{B} \right)$ 
    (ii) $2\text{cosAcosB}=\text{cos}\left( \text{A}-\text{B} \right)+\text{cos}\left( \text{A}+\text{B} \right)$ 
    (iii) $2\text{sinAcosB}=\text{sin}\left( \text{A}+\text{B} \right)+\text{sin}\left( \text{A}-\text{B} \right)$ 
    (iv) $2\text{cosAsinB}=\text{sin}\left( \text{A}+\text{B} \right)-\text{sin}\left( \text{A}-\text{B} \right)$

(b) Then find the integral by using the standard formulae.
